data.title: AMG GW&K ESG Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Drivers

The AMG GW&K ESG Bond Fund (Class N) returned 0.89% in the fourth quarter of 2025, compared with the 1.10% return of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. For the 12 months ending December 31, 2025, the Fund returned 7.26%, compared with the 7.30% return for the benchmark. Please note that this Fund has multiple share classes.

Market Overview

The quarter began with a federal government shutdown, the longest in U.S. history, creating a lack of official data releases and reducing visibility around the Federal Reserve Board's (the Fed's) willingness to lower rates. Despite this uncertainty, the market looked to other available data sources to reinforce a view that a weakening jobs market overshadowed stubbornly high, but moderating inflation. Ultimately, the Fed delivered on their second and third consecutive cuts, stressing their priority to manage near-term labor market risks while striking a less hawkish policy path than feared. The third quarter earnings season broadly exceeded expectations, delivering the fourth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth, painting the picture of an economy that remains resilient. A backdrop of an accommodative Fed, strong corporate fundamentals, and the lowest rate volatility in four years, allowed front-end treasury rates to edge lower while boosting most risk markets.

The Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index returned 1.1% for the quarter, capping off the best annual return since 2020 at 7.3%. The securitized sector led the way with agency fixed rate mortgage-backed securities ('MBS') spreads outperforming, benefiting from the reduced volatility in rates markets. Investment grade corporates managed to produce positive excess returns by offsetting modest spread widening with their additional carry over treasuries. The treasury curve continued to steepen, led by lower front-end rates with the two- and five-year declining 13 and two basis points, respectively, while 10- and 30-year rates were up two and 11 basis points. The 2s30s yield curve ended the year at its steepest level as the front end continued to be anchored by accommodative Fed policy while the long end faced investor fears over inflation, fiscal supply concerns, and U.S. Dollar demand issues.

During the fourth quarter, concerns that idiosyncratic credit losses at lower-quality companies could broaden into systemic issues across loans and private credit contributed to a modest early sell-off. Growing questions about returns on artificial intelligence ('AI') infrastructure investments added pressure before risk markets rebounded on renewed rate cut optimism and moderating inflation. Investment grade spreads ended the quarter at 78 open-adjusted spreads ('OAS'), only a few basis points away from multi-decade lows, returning 0.8%. High yield spreads at 266 OAS were essentially unchanged, with cash conversion cycles (CCCs) underperforming in a reversal of last quarter's strong lower-rated performance. Double-B and single-B spreads were slightly tighter, however, driving the High Yield Index to return 1.3%, slightly outperforming the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index. Corporate credit spreads continue to reflect limited downside risk amid low recession odds and a benign default outlook. Further, macro conditions remain supportive of investor demand and credit fundamentals, with risks more likely credit-specific than macro-related. However, a significant rise in net issuance next year introduces a new technical uncertainty.

Securitized products delivered strong performance in the fourth quarter, led by the outperformance of the agency MBS sector. MBS spreads tightened to multi-year lows as persistently high mortgage rates constrained supply, while a steeper yield curve and declining rate volatility boosted demand. Overall, the outlook for securitized markets remains constructive, as the conclusion of quantitative tightening and the Fed's initiation of reserve management operations in December are supportive of more rangebound rate volatility and sustained demand heading into the new year.

Fund Performance Drivers

The Fund underperformed the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index for the quarter. Underperformance was driven by an out-of-benchmark allocation to high yield and an overweight to corporates. This was partially offset by security selection within corporates, particularly in the BBB-rated technology sector. The Fund's overweight to preferreds were also modest positive contributors. The effects from duration and yield curve positioning were minimal.

Outlook and Positioning

As we enter 2026, we are mindful of this administration's meaningful efforts to further bolster various weaknesses in the economy both through their actions in the capital markets and fiscal policy. At recent times of crisis, the markets have abided by the rule of "don't fight the Fed" and looked for supportive monetary policy actions. Now, we've added other areas of the government to that support, and it has been evidenced with falling volatility in the capital markets. This backdrop looks to continue to be supportive of credit and securitized markets in the coming year, especially as the growth in technological advancements creates productivity gains and alters financial landscapes. Despite fears of global conflicts, an explosion of AI-driven debt supply, and markets that have priced much of the good news in, stable and reasonable fixed-income yields look to make this another year where thoughtful active management can find attractive value in corporates and high-grade securitized products.

data.title: LKQ Corp.: More Things To Be Fixed Before This Can Be A Buy
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on LKQ Corporation (LKQ) was a hold rating because I saw no strong catalyst that would result in multiples getting re-rated upwards. The setup is better than before, in my view. Specialty has clearly improved, and North America continues to take share in a weak market. However, I do not think LKQ has done enough to justify an upgrade since organic growth remains weak, margins are still under pressure, and Europe is not fixed.

4Q25 Results Update

LKQ's Q4 headline revenue number looked better than it really was. Total revenue grew 2.7% y/y on a reported basis, with total parts and services revenue up 2.2%, but that number included a 3.7% FX tailwind. Organically, parts and services revenue was down 1.7%. Splitting by regions, North America remains as the best-performing part (relatively) of the business. Parts and services revenue was down 0.7% organically here, while Europe's organic growth was down 4.8%. By segments, Specialty did really well with organic growth of 7.8%. Other than this, there weren't really any other positives. EBIT fell 40.9% y/y, while segment EBITDA was down to $321 million from $392 million. Net income from continuing operations was also down 50% y/y, and diluted EPS went from $0.58 to $0.29, while adj. diluted EPS fell to $0.59 from $0.78.

Areas That Have Turned Better

Looking ahead, there are reasons to turn optimistic. One of the negative points I made before is that there was no obvious near-term catalyst that could shift sentiment. That is no longer true. While management was not explicit that they would do it, they mentioned the possibility of a potential sale of the company (which is still in process to explore a sale of the Specialty segment). While the timing of this happening is virtually impossible to know, it does help with the stock sentiment (forming a valuation floor). On the part about the sale of Specialty, the value of it should have increased since Q4 saw the second consecutive quarter of organic growth. LKQ is no longer trying to sell a segment that looks like it is collapsing. The operating trend is better now, which at least improves the valuation during any sale process.

North America also looks better from here. We now have more evidence that LKQ's share gains are real, and I would expect more share gains from here. The key line that led me to this view is that management said LKQ gained share by deepening relationships with MSOs (multi-shop operators) and insurers, and they also said repairable claims improved from ~-10% in 1Q to -4% to -6% in Q4. This tells us that the market was still obviously weak, but things have improved over the months, and that sets up easier comps for FY2026 (especially while LKQ is still winning wallet share). On top of that, management said volumes sold to MSO customers were up in the teens in FY2025, while MSO volumes overall (industry level) were only flat to slightly higher (i.e., LKQ gained a ton of share here).

Reason to Stay Neutral

The main reason is that this business is still not showing signs of growth. Organic revenue growth is down across both regions, and total segment EBITDA margin contracted by 240 bps to 9.7%. We can point out all the positive developments underneath the hood, but if the P&L is not showing, I don't think the market will attach any value to them. This is especially the case for Europe, where management said that demand weakness was due to soft consumer confidence, macro uncertainty, and pricing pressure. The first two are not new since Europe has been in this uncertain territory for the recent history, but pricing pressure is something new to me. It suggests that demand must have deteriorated significantly, so much that LKQ has to cut prices to capture volume.

The fortunate news for the bulls is that LKQ has acted accordingly. They expanded private-label inventory with introductory pricing (which helps with lowering effective prices while protecting margins), reviewed >85% of the Europe SKU portfolio, delisted 71,000 SKUs, and said a key system integration is planned for early 2Q26. If all goes well, we should see Europe starting to print positive comps. LKQ also kickstarted a restructuring plan that is expected to generate more than $50 million of annualized cost savings. String all these together, and I would say the setup is better than my last write-up. But the problem has not gone away. Management also said these pricing and private-label actions are pressuring revenue and margins in the near term. So, Europe has a visible path to recovery today, but it is not yet easier to underwrite.

Valuation

As discussed, there are reasons to lean bullish after this Q4 performance. We finally have a proper catalyst in sight; Specialty is proving to be rebounding nicely, LKQ continues to win share in North America, and there is a plan to turn things around in Europe. It makes sense that the market reacted positively, pushing valuation up from 9.5x NTM PE to the ~10.5x today. I won't challenge that view, but from here, I think the path from 10.5x to 13x (the historical average) will need more proper execution. LKQ must show tangible improvements in organic growth and margin (both of which are still poor today). It is also hard to expect multiples to go any higher when one of LKQ's peers, Genuine Parts Company (GPC), trades at 15x with mid-single-digit revenue growth expectations (LKQ is at low-single-digits).

Conclusion

My view for LKQ remains a hold rating. I think this quarter was better than the last in a few ways. LKQ now has a catalyst, Specialty is no longer a drag, and North America share gains are real and should last. But the main issue is that the improvement still is not showing up clearly enough in the P&L. Organic growth is still negative, margins are still weak, and Europe remains the biggest overhang.

data.title: PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Core Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equities posted modest gains for the quarter, closing 2025 firmly in positive territory.

Inflation remained above target, but the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered its target rate twice during the quarter, prompted by a softening labor market.

Unprofitable artificial intelligence ('AI')-related companies initially attracted investor enthusiasm early in the quarter before experiencing a sharp revaluation in November, signaling a shift away from speculative growth towards fundamentals.

Despite late-December weakness in major indexes, the Russell 2000 returned just over 2% for the quarter, and the S&P 500 and S&P 400 indexes returned 2.66% and 1.64%, respectively.

Strong factor performance in the large-cap space boosted relative returns. However, value and growth factors weighed on relative performance in small-cap portfolios. Factor performance was mixed in the mid-cap space.

Quarter

After a turbulent start driven by global trade tensions, investors remained in risk-on mode for much of the year, fueled by easing monetary policy, robust corporate earnings, and AI hype. Following a strong January, U.S. equities turned markedly negative amid escalating trade war tensions, culminating in a broad market sell-off in early April. Markets then rallied meaningfully as trade tensions eased, setting the stage for U.S. equities to climb to new record highs for the remainder of the year, despite the persistent realities of stretched valuations and above-target inflation.

Monetary policy remained a key driver of market volatility as investors anticipated the Fed's next move. Price pressures lingered throughout the year, complicating policymakers' efforts to support economic growth while simultaneously tempering inflation. The consumer price index ('CPI') began the year near 3%, fluctuated throughout the year, and stood at 2.7% in November, still above the Fed's long-run 2% target. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate increased by 0.5% year-over-year to 4.6% in November, its highest level since 2021. Coupled with a slowdown in job growth, the softening labor market was the primary catalyst for the Fed's three rate cuts in the second half of the year.

Market leadership remained narrow. Despite underperforming the broad market during the first quarter, mega-cap stocks, most notably Alphabet (GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA), rebounded sharply and ultimately outpaced the broader index. Strong earnings and the monetization of AI initiatives were key drivers of the outperformance.

By November, this small group of stocks reached historically elevated levels of concentration, accounting for approximately 36% of the S&P 500 Index. Valuation dispersion also remained wide, with the forward-looking Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiple of the Russell 1000 Growth Index nearly double that of the Value Index. Despite this highly concentrated environment, our large-cap strategy fared well.

The period was unique for small-cap equities. Following a weak start to the year, small-cap stocks outperformed large-cap stocks during the third quarter, fueled by optimism surrounding easing monetary policy. In July, investors rotated into low-quality, speculative stocks, sparking a short-lived junk rally. The rally broadened in September and October as investors shifted into unprofitable tech companies, hoping to capture AI-related growth tailwinds. These stocks reversed during November as concerns grew around a potential bubble. Despite this correction, non-earners within the Russell 2000 Index outperformed their positive-earning counterparts by more than 10% for the full year, a phenomenon last observed in 2020.

Though the third quarter market rally was broad, mega-cap tech stocks remained the front-runners, driven by AI enthusiasm and strong earnings results. The Russell 1000 Growth Index returned over 10%, compared to around 5% for the Russell 1000 Value Index. The valuation disparity widened for the quarter, with the forward-looking P/E multiple of the Russell 1000 Growth Index rising above 35, almost double that of the Value Index.

Factor efficacy was challenged during the first quarter and into early April, as investors indiscriminately sold stocks regardless of fundamentals amid heightened market noise driven by ongoing tariff developments. Investors rotated out of expensive tech and into low volatility stocks, reflecting a risk-off mentality. These conditions were particularly difficult to navigate in our large- and mid-cap strategies. In the small-cap space, value and quality factors experienced notable periods of underperformance, particularly in September and October, when low-quality, expensive stocks led returns. This dynamic also weighed on mid-cap returns. Such market conditions present headwinds for an alpha-seeking, diversified strategy like ours. Factor performance improved into year end, particularly in the large-cap space, where value factors staged a notable rebound as investors rotated into undervalued areas of the market.

The year as a whole presented no shortage of challenges, marked by bouts of volatility, heightened concentration, and speculative rallies. Against this backdrop, our small- and mid-cap portfolios lagged their respective benchmarks, while our large-cap portfolios trailed modestly for the year.

Despite these short-term headwinds, relative performance has been very strong over the trailing five years, reflecting the durability of our investment process across varying market environments.

Looking ahead, uncertainty persists across several fronts, most notably from geopolitical tensions, the pace of Fed easing, and the sustainability of AI-driven revenue growth. Nevertheless, we firmly believe our portfolios are well-positioned to navigate these evolving market conditions.

Outlook and Positioning

PGIM Quantitative Solutions employs a quantitative, bottom-up investment process. Our proprietary stock selection model utilizes a carefully chosen suite of value, growth and quality factors with strong theoretical underpinnings to evaluate a broad universe of stocks. Our process is adaptive. Factors are dynamically weighted to reflect differences in individual stock growth rates. This ensures we are examining stocks through the right lenses.

Using the relative scores from the stock selection model, PGIM Quantitative Solutions constructs a Fund that efficiently targets factor exposures, while minimizing unintended risks and transactions costs. The result is a diversified core Fund that is positioned to produce consistent alpha over time.

data.title: USANA Looks Like An Undervalued Turnaround Stock
data.description:
USANA Health Sciences (USNA) is in the middle of a turnaround. USANA’s main business segment, its MLM, may be in permanent decline. In general, MLMs aren’t doing very well right now. So, like other MLMs, the company is pivoting. USANA has created two other businesses, its Hiya direct-to-customer brand and its Rise Wellness brand, that are rapidly growing. The company appears to be using its core MLM segment as a cash cow to fund its growth businesses, and its Q4 2025 results indicate that this strategy is working.

Because of Hiya and Rise Wellness, the company as a whole reported growing revenue in 2025. In Q4 2025, USANA reported that its revenue rose 5.9% to $226.2 million. The company also reported a GAAP loss of $1.8 million for the quarter, but it also reported $27.8 million in adjusted EBITDA. USANA is incurring additional expenses right now because its business model is changing, so I think it is appropriate to use adjusted EBITDA for this company. For example, USANA reported $7.0 million in impairment charges for the quarter, which is a non-cash expense, and it’s also larger than the GAAP loss the company reported. So, on a non-GAAP basis, this company is still profitable.

It’s likely that USANA will report a profit in 2026 as well. While SA says that Wall Street analysts aren’t covering this stock right now, it currently has a forward P/E of 10. So, right now this could be a growth stock that’s priced like a value stock. USANA’s new business segments are already generating growth, and the company has some big partners that will help it continue to generate growth in 2026.

Hiya is Collaborating With Disney to Make Kids’ Vitamins

USANA’s Hiya subsidiary appears to have a long-term collaboration deal with Disney (DIS). According to MediaPost, Hiya launched Lion King vitamins in the spring of 2025 and then launched Disney Princess vitamins during the summer. While these might sound like limited-time offers, I visited Hiya’s website, and the Disney-branded vitamins are still available there. So, while management didn’t say anything about upcoming collaborations on the Q4 2025 call, there may be more collaborations between Disney and Hiya in the future. The Hiya vitamins are clean-label products that are sweetened with sugar alternatives, so the brand is on-trend.

And unlike some of the other companies I’ve covered recently, USANA is unlikely to disappoint investors with a collaboration that doesn’t perform well. Kids need to take multivitamins on a regular schedule, so vitamins aren’t a one-time purchase. Additionally, Hiya is selling its vitamins through a subscription service. So, customers who order vitamins from this company are likely to order them again. As of the end of Q4 2025, Hiya has 181,700 subscribers.

USANA reported that Hiya generated $30 million in sales during Q4 2025 and expects Hiya to report full-year 2026 sales in the $140-$155 million range. So, Hiya could generate $120 million in annualized sales right now based on its fourth-quarter results and is expected to generate $147.5 million in annual sales at the midpoint in 2026. That’s a 23% revenue growth rate.

Rise Wellness is Also Gaining Wider Distribution for Protein Pop

USANA also expects Rise Wellness to contribute $65-$80 million to its revenue in 2026. This brand’s main product is Protein Pop, a carbonated drink with whey protein and collagen. It looks like Protein Pop could scale up very quickly because USANA has already convinced Costco (COST) and Target (TGT) to stock the drinks. So, this is another new product that could make a significant contribution to USANA’s revenue in 2026.

Meanwhile, the company’s inventory of protein drinks is rising because it’s preparing to distribute these drinks to the stores. This is another reason why the company’s recent GAAP results might be misleading. It’s carrying more inventory than it did in previous years, and that includes the Hiya vitamins that are now stored in its warehouses as well. USANA’s inventory rose from $69.7 million in Q4 2024 to $102.6 million in Q4 2025.

The Core USANA Business is Still Shrinking

In January 2026, USANA released preliminary guidance that implies that its core segment, its MLM, will report weaker sales in 2026. The company said that its core segment generated sales of around $777 million in 2025 but will generate sales of $720 to $765 million in 2026. So, at the midpoint, the company expects its core segment sales to fall 4% to $742.5 million.

My thesis for USANA is that the growth from Hiya and Rise Wellness will make up for the declining sales of USANA’s core MLM segment. This is similar to my long-term thesis for Simply Good Foods (SMPL), which owns the shrinking Atkins brand as well as the growing Quest and OWYN brands. While I still think Simply Good Foods has good long-term growth potential because of this setup, the company is experiencing margin compression right now, and that’s also a risk factor for USANA.

USANA’s Pivot is a Risk Factor

USANA’s core segment still operates as an MLM, but now the company is selling vitamins directly to its customers, and its protein drinks are available at major retail stores. In its earnings report, the company also described its MLM segment as a legacy channel. Specifically, the USANA CEO said, “We are leveraging our strong foundation in the nutrition business and expanding beyond our legacy channel to connect with a greater number of health-conscious consumers worldwide.”

USANA also used to refer to its MLM members as associates, and then it changed that to brand partners in Q2 2025. Now, it sounds like it’s calling them active customers. In the Q4 2025 report, the company said it has 387,000 active customers, down from 454,000 a year ago. So, it sounds like USANA plans to transition away from the MLM model. This could be a good long-term move for the company, but some of its MLM members might be unhappy about this. If these members leave earlier than expected, USANA might report weaker results in 2026.

Comparative Valuation for USANA

Because of the uncertainty involved with its pivot, this company is trading at very low valuations right now. After the market close on March 3, USANA had a forward EV/EBITDA of 2.67 and a forward P/FCF ratio of 7.31. It’s also trading at a P/B ratio of 0.69. While other MLMs are also trading at low valuations right now, USANA is still trading at a discount to peers. Herbalife’s forward EV/EBITDA is 5.58, and Nature’s Sunshine has a forward EV/EBITDA of 8.53.

My USANA Valuation

In the fourth quarter, USANA reported an adjusted EBITDA margin of 12.3%. It’s likely that because of its change in business model, as well as rising input costs, its margins will be lower in 2026. So, I’ll project that USANA’s EBITDA margin will drop 200 basis points to 10.3% in 2026. The midpoint of its guidance calls for $962.5 million in revenue, so I’d project that its EBITDA will be $99.1 million in 2026. This is actually lower than the company’s EBITDA guidance for 2026, but I’m being conservative here.

But this company is also trading at a massive discount to other MLMs and food companies, so I’d project that its EV/EBITDA will rise to 3.5 in 2026 as well. That gives me a projected EV of $347 million. USANA’s capital structure is also a bit more complex than other companies’ capital structures. At the end of Q4 2025, USANA had $158 million in cash. The company had no long-term debt, but it did have a $14 million line of credit liability. And it also reported $53 million in redeemable non-controlling interest because of the 2024 Hiya acquisition. Non-controlling shareholders still have a 21.2% stake in Hiya and will have the right to force USANA to buy their remaining stake in the future. So, if I add USANA’s cash back to its EV and subtract its line of credit balance and the non-controlling interest, I get a 2026 market cap projection of $438 million.

USANA has also been buying back stock, and its diluted share count has dropped from 25 million shares in 2016 to 18.6 million shares in 2025. So, I’ll project that USANA’s share count will drop to 18.0 million at the end of 2026 as well. After dividing a market cap of $438 million by 18.0 million shares, I get a one-year target price of $24.33 per share. USANA’s closing price on March 3 was $19.45 per share, so my target price is 25% higher than the current price.

Again, Wall Street analysts aren’t covering this stock right now, but SA analysts give it a hold rating.

Conclusion

USANA’s pivot appears to be working. While the company’s core MLM segment is slowly shrinking and will probably continue to shrink, its two growth segments have the potential to grow rapidly. Hiya, in particular, appears to be a very good brand. And Rise Wellness could also succeed because of rising demand for protein drinks and expanded distribution in 2026. Meanwhile, USANA is trading at a very low valuation and has a strong balance sheet. While this company’s future does appear uncertain, it seems like the reward could outweigh the risks here. So, I say that USANA is a buy.

data.title: UVXY And SVXY: Trading Volatility In A Turbulent Market
data.description:
The ProShares Ultra VIX Short-Term Futures ETF (UVXY) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with amplified exposure to the daily performance of the S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures Index. With the current market turmoil and geopolitical tensions building, I believe volatility will remain heightened for the time being, providing investors with an opportunity to exploit short-term dispersions using UVXY. Strategically, traders may balance the upside and downside risk with the ProShares Short VIX Short-Term Futures ETF (SVXY) to capture the decline in volatility. Given the speculative nature of volatility and the leveraged risk involved in these strategies, traders should solely utilize these ETFs for daily exposure; holding leveraged strategies for longer than a single trading day may expose investors to substantial and potentially unrecoverable losses.

You can review my ETF Educational coverage of UVXY here and SVXY here. Investors should note that these reports were not rated and were purely intended for educational purposes.

How to Balance the Trade

Forecasting a spike in volatility and determining the sustainability of heightened volatility may not necessarily be as simple as capturing the decline in volatility; however, we can potentially build a trading strategy designed to capture upside potential on either side of the jump in volatility.

Over time, volatility generally normalizes at a low level as markets settle and generally trend upward in the long run. With this assumption baked into our trading strategy, we can infer that capturing returns by taking a position in SVXY [short volatility] can be somewhat more predictable when compared to forecasting a jump in volatility. I’m not going to go as far as to say that anytime there’s a spike in volatility, an investor should immediately take a short position, as we cannot assume that the initial spike is the end of the upward momentum. I’m more so referring to the long-term remediation of volatility, in which, when following a large increase, volatility will normalize at a lower rate over time. This phenomenon can be observed following the market decline in 2025 following the release of DeepSeek-V3 in late March 2025.

Observing the spike in volatility, the increase was near immediate, with churning market action following, testing the upper end before phasing out over time.

In similar fashion, the volatility index spiked on March 3, 2026, as a result of heightened geopolitical tensions and the emergence of military action in the Middle East, which we will reference but not further discuss in this article. With the immediate jump in volatility, traders must question whether this is the height of the increase or whether volatility will continue rising following the cooling-off period.

One indicator that may be considered is the oil market’s reaction to the military action, with West Texas Intermediate [WTI] prices rising above $70/bbl for the first time since July 2025. Oil prices are expected to rise and potentially remain elevated during the military action occurring in the Middle East, as the Strait of Hormuz is a critical bottleneck for oil and LNG cargoes leaving the Middle East for Asian and European markets. While the initial reaction was substantial, I suspect that the upward price momentum is far from over.

Natural gas price increases began globally with minor initial price adjustments for Henry Hub natural gas. I believe that the lagging impact on domestic gas prices will be overlooked as European and Asian markets will now become more dependent on US LNG cargoes. This, in turn, is potentially leading to a greater price normalization for domestic prices, meaning that more producers may divert gas capacity to excess LNG export offtake in an attempt to realize stronger prices.

While the energy sector only accounts for 3.47% of the S&P 500 Index (SPY), the ramifications of higher oil and gas prices can create fear amongst investors and consumers amid the relative impact of higher costs for gasoline and natural gas. Investors must not forget that we’re still in the cold weather season and that consumers dependent on affordable natural gas for heating may become cost-sensitive to their discretionary budgets.

As these factors become rationalized in the market, there very well may be another sharp increase in volatility, particularly if the situation in the Middle East worsens. While this may lead to negative performance in the S&P 500, investors may hedge downward momentum using UVXY for short-term, daily exposure to capture the market’s downside risk.

In contrast, I have built-in expectations that the S&P 500 will be relatively flat, in aggregate, for 2026, meaning that I expect the broader Index to remain relatively range-bound with no directional momentum. Given this assumption, I believe any sharp increase in volatility will be followed by a normalization down, creating an opportunity for traders to utilize SVXY to capture this decline.

From an active trading perspective, I believe traders should wait until the volatility index normalizes down before buying into a position, given my outlook for the S&P 500. Given the leveraged risk in UVXY, holding the ETF during a decline in volatility can significantly impact one’s position and result in substantial losses during the volatility normalization period. Overall, the buy/sell signals for UVXY and SVXY are as follows:

Long SVXY following a large increase in volatility. Long UVXY only after the volatility index normalizes down to minimize risk.

As simple as this may seem, investors should bear in mind that this is entirely dependent on my expectations for the S&P 500 trading relatively flat for 2026 and that volatility will be more dependent on exogenous effects rather than on fundamentals.

About UVXY and SVXY

UVXY provides traders with 1.5x the daily performance of the S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures Index. UVXY has a net expense ratio of 95bps. The ETF provides traders with substantial liquidity with $363mm in net assets and an average of $326mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis.

SVXY provides traders with -0.5x the daily performance of the S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures Index. SVXY has a net expense ratio of 95bps. The fund has more modest liquidity relative to UVXY, with $191mm in net assets and an average of $106mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis.

Risks to Consider

Leveraged strategies should only be held for daily exposure; holding these funds for longer than a single trading day may result in compounded returns and may as well result in substantial, unsalvageable losses if inappropriately applied. With both SVXY and UVXY employing futures positions to gain exposure to the Index, investors may face NAV decay over time as a result of fees associated with the futures positions. Futures derivatives are settled on a daily basis, resulting in a performance reset that may be reflected in the ETFs' performance that may compound returns if held for longer than a single trading day.

An example of the compounding effect is that if starting with $100, a 1% gain in UVXY would result in the investor closing the trading day with $101.5. If the following trading day also has a 1% gain, the investor will close the trading day with $103.0225, a 1.5% gain on the $101.5, not the initial $100. The same can be said of the downside. If UVXY were to follow these two days with two consecutive days of -1% losses, the investment will decline to $99.955 and not $100.

Investors should consider liquidity and trading depth prior to placing a trade in these strategies. Thinner liquidity may make entering and exiting a position more costly.

Investors should also consider all fees associated with trading leveraged strategies, including the expense ratio, brokerage transaction fees, spread risk, and short-term capital gains taxes.

Given the risky nature of leveraged strategies, traders should adhere to strict risk management protocols and sell discipline in order to protect their positions. UVXY and SVXY should only be used by experienced traders; the misuse of these ETFs can result in substantial losses.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

Despite my expectations of flat performance in the S&P 500 for 2026, exogenous impacts to the market can create significant increases in short-term volatility that can be actively traded, facilitated through the use of SVXY and UVXY. While volatility generally normalizes to a lower rate over time, heightened geopolitical risk and market disruptions can create opportunities for UVXY, while volatility normalization can create opportunities for positive SVXY returns. Given the neutral nature of long-term volatility, I am recommending SVXY and UVXY with Hold ratings.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

data.title: Dassault Aviation: Rafale Export Momentum Is Driving The Bull Case
data.description:
Dassault Aviation (DUAVF) stock has surged 33.6% since my last report, in which I maintained my buy rating. Driven by positive momentum for aerospace due to global unrest, the European defense autonomy initiative, and an overarching supercycle for the aerospace and defense industry, Dassault Aviation outperformed against a flat market. In this report, I discuss Dassault Aviation’s full-year earnings published on the fourth of March, and I update my price target in support of my buy rating.

Dassault Aviation Earnings Grow Strongly With Modest Margin Expansion

Order intake remained stable in 2025 at €10.9 billion. We note that Falcon order intake increased in terms of aircraft volumes but was stable in terms of value. Defense export orders declined by 4 units but increased 2.7% in terms of value, while defense orders from France declined by 20% to €0.8 billion. While order intake did not show growth, we note that on a 19% increase in sales, the book-to-bill ratio still remained above one, signalling growth opportunities from a demand perspective. The book-to-bill ratio for the year was 1.5x compared to 1.75 a year ago, and backlog increased 8% to €46.6 billion. We note that the share of defense export backlog grew from 68% to 73%. Export sales tend to come at a higher margin, so if the backlog translates favorably into the revenue mix, we could see some margin expansion.

Sales rose 19% to €7.4 billion with 21.7% growth for Falcon business jet deliveries and services, 50% growth in defense exports, and a 15.8% decline for defense sales to France. Adjusted operating income grew from €519 million to €635 million, marking 22.5% growth and a 30-basis point margin expansion from 8.3% to 8.6%. Adjusted net income remained stable at €1.061, driven by the tax surcharge in France adding €96 million in taxes. This more than offset positive contributions from Thales.

Dassault Guides for €8.5 Billion in Sales

For 2026, Dassault Aviation has guided for €8.5 billion in sales, assuming that Falcon jet deliveries will increase by three units to 40 and Rafale fighter jet deliveries will increase by two units to 28. So, we are not seeing major upticks in volumes, but I believe that each delivery the company adds should drive operating leverage further amplified by positive margin effects from export sales.

The Risks and Opportunities for Dassault

Dassault Aviation has growth prospects with exposure in the commercial and defense markets. In the commercial market, higher delivery figures and services support should be a positive with the Falcon 10X set to be introduced in 2027. As we close in on the introduction, it does not only bring the sales prospects for the 10X closer but also drive a reduction in Falcon 10X-related R&D costs.

In the defense market, there are prospects for the Rafale fighter jet. Export sales generally come at higher margins and also create long-term revenue tails for sustainment. However, one risk for those sales is simply losing out on sales opportunities. Several countries are trying to sell their fighter jets to air forces around the world, and usually this is a matter of “winner takes it all.” So, there are big opportunities, but losing out means that fighter jet manufacturers lose sales prospects with a single customer for decades. Another risk is the FCAS or Future Combat Air System. This multinational, multi-company effort to bring a new fighter jet to the market is not rolling smoothly due to friction between companies and countries. Dassault Aviation is the primary contractor on the program, but so far the paths and timelines for the program are under pressure, just like the entire program and cooperation.

Dassault Aviation Stock Price Target Increased

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Dassault Aviation, I am increasing my price target from $380 to $484.16, indicating 20% upside. This is driven by an increase in the peer group trading multiple from 21.3x to 22.8x. EBITDA came in 2.3% higher than expected in 2025, while free cash flow was 4.5% better than expected. In the years ahead, sales are expected to grow 13.4% annually, with 19.5% annual growth in EBITDA. We note that free cash flow does not show a meaningful direction due to the timing of advance payments on military orders. However, we note that the company is in a net cash position, which is definitely a rarity for aerospace and defense companies, and in my view adds to the appeal.

In the years ahead, sales are expected to continue growing with a strong improvement in the EBITDA margins driven by improved operating leverage. Capital expenditures are expected to be more or less stable, but we do note that CapEx was substantially lower in 2025. If this is set to be a new baseline, that frees up some cash. Cash flow performance is expected to be volatile, driven by the timing of advance payments. These advance payments hinge on winning sales campaigns and the timing of contract signing. So, there is some upside for free cash flow in case we see additional contracts being signed in the years ahead. It also highlights that free cash flow performance for Dassault does not necessarily reflect operational performance but also combines order inflow, which can substantially improve the profile.

Conclusion: Dassault Aviation Stock Remains Attractive

I believe that Dassault Aviation remains attractive. There are opportunities to sell fighter jets globally as tension is reaching new highs. At the same time, we are also cognizant of the tension on the FCAS program. It is a program on which Dassault is the prime contractor. On paper, this is a pan-European development effort that should have major financial prospects, but tensions are infusing the program with substantial risk. I am maintaining my buy rating based on growing prospects for sales in the commercial and defense end markets.

data.title: Cushman & Wakefield: Focus On The Fundamentals, And Not AI Narrative Noise
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Cushman & Wakefield (CWK) earlier this year, with my key thesis being that this is a business that will continue to see stable demand and that it has a strong tailwind from AI/data center demand. My view remains bullish. Capital markets and leasing both stayed strong, which tells me underlying real estate activity is improving.

Earnings Results Update

CWK reported a strong Q4 2025 quarter with total revenue growth of 11% y/y to $2.9 billion, while service line fee revenue grew 9% y/y to $2.04 billion. Growth was evident across the board. Services revenue grew 8% y/y, leasing revenue grew 6%, capital markets revenue grew 17%, and valuation and other revenue was up 15%. On the top line numbers alone, this was a solid quarter. However, things were a little mixed at the bottom line. GAAP net income went into a net loss of $22.4 million (from $112.9 million last year). This was mainly due to the $177 million other-than-temporary impairment related to the Greystone JV. But if we look at this on an adjusted basis, it is not that bad. Adj. net income was up 12% y/y to $127.1 million, and adj. diluted EPS was $0.54, up 13%.

Capital Markets Suggest Underlying Demand is Improving

This was the fifth consecutive quarter of double-digit y/y growth for the capital markets segment, which I think should continue, as this was driven by improved debt availability and flow through of deals that were held up previously due to lack of funding. In the call, management also said capital markets still have room to run if the macro backdrop avoids any dramatic shock. That tells me that we are still in the early innings of the growth runway. Also, the strength here isn't really just in one region. CWK is seeing strength across major regions. Americas grew 19%; EMEA grew 19% on a reported basis and 9% in local currency; and while APAC was down 6%, it was mainly due to a difficult comparison in Japan.

The more important implication here is underlying real estate activity is improving. Beneath all real estate transactions is really the health of the capital markets. My view is simple: when buyers can get financing and deals start moving through the pipelines again, it suggests the commercial real estate backdrop is starting to improve, and that ties to the leasing part of the CWK business that I will talk about next.

Leasing Strength

Leasing was another strong part of the quarter. While revenue growth was only ~6% y/y, it was supported by positive growth across all regions. Especially in the Americas, growth was supported by strength in office and industrial, a higher deal count, and higher revenue per lease. This lines up with the capital markets point I made above. In capital markets, management talked about better debt availability and stronger transaction activity. In leasing, they talked about higher deal counts and better revenue per lease. If you put those together, the message is pretty simple, and that is activity in commercial real estate is picking up. Management also said this was the highest Q4 leasing result in CWK's history, which further solidifies my point that demand activity is recovering.

I think an important element here is what is driving that recovery. In industrial, demand is still largely focused on facilities that can support automation and higher power needs. My read here is this suggests demand for more complex assets, and those are the kinds of requirements that usually need more advisory work and support. In other words, the CWK value proposition goes up, and so should revenue per lease. Also, demand isn't just coming from new construction builds but also from upgrading existing assets. I believe management talked about this when they mentioned "project management opportunities," and that makes sense. When a client leases or upgrades an office, industrial site, or other property, CWK has a chance to capture that activity since it has handled the initial transaction.

Put together, the capital market strength and underlying drivers for leasing growth suggest that we should continue to see CWK printing healthy growth rates in the coming quarters.

AI Tools

The AI angle is still early, but I think a few of the tools management mentioned are worth paying attention to. The first is the AI impact barometer. This seems useful, as it can help investors and occupiers make better decisions, as AI may shift demand across property types, locations, and building requirements over time. The second is the capital markets CRM, which should help to get data flowing more freely across CWK internally. The latter probably has more direct impact in the near term, as successful cross-selling shows up in the P&L straight away. My view is that if teams can see more of the client relationship and work off the same data, it becomes easier to sell more than one service line to the same account. The last tool to mention is OneAdvise in leasing. This basically helps automate digital tour books, lease negotiations, benchmarks, and space planning. All of these are essential parts of an advisor workflow, but they are time-consuming. By automating, then, it should free up advisors' time for more higher-value client work (like advising on complex lease decisions, cross-selling, project management, etc.)

To sum it up, these tools are important not because AI is driving huge revenue growth today, but because they can make CWK more useful to clients, improve how the platform works internally, and make cross-selling easier over time.

Valuation

If you have been following what's happening, you would know that Anthropic's Claude destroyed billions of equity value across various sectors in February, and one of those "victims" is the real estate service sector (which CWK is inside). The entire sector valuation fell from >11x to ~10x forward EBITDA today, and the CWK valuation fell from >8x to ~6.7x. I believe the market has overestimated the negative impact of AI and is not giving credit to CWK fundamentals. The reality is underlying demand is recovering. It also doesn't make sense to me that one could potentially "vibe-code" CWK's business because this is not a simple online matching business. Have a deep think through of how complex some of these deals are and how much they can be worth (so much financial and operational risk involved). Buyers are making 5- to 10-year decisions, and I doubt they would rely on vibe-coded software (or whatever it is). The right framing should be that AI is helpful for CWK as it helps with information flow and internal efficiency. I think once CWK continues to print healthy numbers, multiples should snap back to ~8x or so easily, which is a 20% upside (on just multiple revision).

Investment Risk

My worry would be on near-term margin weakness. Q4 adjusted EBITDA margin fell 16 bps due to cost inflation, which offset some of the revenue growth strength. If, for whatever reason, this keeps happening, the stock sentiment may get further beaten down as investors believe AI is hurting margins. The $177 million Greystone impairment, while it is a non-operating event, is still a reminder that not every investment CWK has worked on has gone as planned. If we see more of this, the market may start to doubt CWK's capital allocation plans.

Conclusion

My positive view on CWK is because the core setup looks better than what the current valuation is implying. My take is that underlying demand is recovering, capital markets still seem to have room to run, and leasing strength suggests activity is picking up. I also think the market is overestimating the risk that AI disrupts this business. In my view, AI is more likely to improve CWK's workflow, cross-selling, and client value proposition. If it keeps printing healthy growth, I believe the stock should rerate from here.

data.title: Core Natural Resources: Operational Normalization Shifts To 2026; Shares Near Fair Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The report for Q4 and the whole of 2025 turned out to be rather weak for Core Natural Resources (CNR), although it managed to beat market expectations in terms of revenue. Downtime in the mines and one-time expenses led to operating losses, which negatively affected stock prices after the financial statements were released. My view of the company has not changed, and I maintain a Hold rating, as I believe that the market price of the shares is close to their fair value, and the drivers for growth, in my opinion, are limited. I updated my DCF model and got a target price of $82 per share with a downside of 11% to the current price ($92 at the time of writing). At the same time, I do not recommend selling the shares, as I do not see strong reasons for their fall in price, but short-term corrections of 5-10% are quite possible. At this price, it is much more reasonable to continue to hold the shares in the portfolio and buy them back at dips.

Q4 Earnings Miss: Weak Margins Offset Strong Revenue Performance

Before proceeding to the analysis of the financial statements for Q4, I recommend studying my first article about the company, where I analyzed in detail the history of its formation, business model, and development strategy.

Reporting for Q4 was worse than expected, primarily due to weak data on the profitability and marginality of the business. However, there are some positive aspects. Revenue increased by 81% yoy to $1.04 billion. This strong growth was due to the effect of business mergers. The consensus forecast for the quarter was as much as 4.6% lower than the final result. As a result, the company earned $4.16 billion throughout the year, which is 89% higher than yoy, again due to the effect of the merger. Revenue for the quarter was higher than market expectations 3 times out of 4.

Nevertheless, the report was interpreted by the market as negative. All thanks to the resulting loss and reduced operating margin. Adjusted EBITDA was $103.1 million, down 27% from Q3. The group's EBITDA margin decreased to about 10% from about 14% in Q3. There are several reasons why the operating results have sunk so much. In the core metallurgical segment, the average selling price was $105.45 per ton at a cost of $103.49. Even under such conditions, it is difficult to expect a high operating profit. This happened due to the fact that the longwall complex on Leer South was idle for most of the quarter, and the restart took place only in mid-December. In the high-calorie thermal segment, the situation turned out to be slightly better: the average selling price was $58.11 per ton at a cost of $41.42, which provided a margin of about $17 per ton. But this result was also insufficient, as the margin was higher in Q3. The average price was weighed down by weaker export pricing and high asset maintenance costs. At Powder River Basin, with a price of $14.21 and a cost of $13.62, the margin remained minimal, which limited the segment's contribution to the overall result.

All of these factors resulted in a net loss of $79 million, or $1.54 per share. The market was very disappointed by the numbers it saw. The consensus forecast was $0.59. For the full year, the company earned a net loss of $153.2 million, or $2.98 per share. At the same time, management actively bought shares from the market, which smoothed the decline in EPS. The annual result was influenced, among other things, by an increase in interest expenses to $40 million. I was wrong with my expectations, as I expected profitability to recover already in Q4 as the synergies from the merger and normalization of production at Leer South were realized, but these effects were actually postponed to 2026. Consider the forecasts of management.

2026 Outlook: Margin Recovery Backed by Operational Normalization and Market Support

At the call, management strongly repeated that the weakness of Q4 was due to one-time factors that should not be repeated in 2026. According to them, the integration of CONSOL and Arch is almost complete, and a synergistic effect of $150-170 million in annual savings is expected. The company plans to bring both Leer South and West Elk mines to full capacity, which will restore business margins. In addition, management expects an improvement in market conditions, which will also support the growth of financial indicators in 2026. According to their estimates, in 2025, the demand for thermal coal increased by 12%, and, in general, coal-fired generation continues to recover amid an increasing load on the energy system. According to the company's estimates, a balance of supply and demand has formed in the metallurgical coal market due to a reduction in supply from high-cost producers and supply disruptions from Australia. Since December, prices for premium coking coal have increased by about 25% to about $250 per ton, which means improvements in operating profit from the first quarter of 2026. It is important that Core approached 2026 with a high level of pre-concluded contracts, which reduces uncertainty about prices and revenue - by the end of 2025, about 78% of future sales had been contracted. In addition to the forecasts of operational indicators, a forecast was given for capital expenditures for the whole of 2026 at the level of $325-375 million, or 8% of revenue. The effective tax rate is expected to be low, about 0-5%.

Against this background, it is interesting to look at the consensus forecasts. The market expects revenue growth in Q1 of 4.54% yoy to $1.06 billion and EPS of $0.44. For the year, consensus expects revenue growth of 5.16% yoy to $4.38 billion and EPS of $4.35. The market as a whole agrees with management and expects margin recovery in the first quarter. In my opinion, the forecasts are quite realistic and feasible. In terms of revenue, Core may even outpace the market forecast. I expect the company to earn around $1.07-1.09 billion and EPS of $0.43-0.46 in Q1.

DCF and Multiples Converge Around Current Price

In this chapter, I will talk about my DCF model, its prerequisites, and final evaluation. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, and then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the market cap; with EV/EBITDA, this is the Enterprise value from which Net Debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

I have revised my financial model towards greater conservatism of the assumptions embedded in the model. According to my forecast, the revenue CAGR for 2026-2030 will be 2.62%. In my opinion, the revenue growth rate will gradually slow down after the main projects reach full capacity in 2026-2027 and with stable coal prices. The operating margin will grow to 12.5% by 2023 due to synergistic effects, but it will not be able to grow more due to rising production costs. Capital expenditures will be kept at 8% of revenue, which will help maintain current assets in good condition and invest in the future. Thus, according to my model, the average FCF Yield from 2026 to 2030 will be about 14.92%.

The WACC has dropped to 5.49% since the July article. This is mainly due to the lower level of the five-year Beta and Risk-free rate.

By multiples, comparing with sector averages, I can't say that the company is strongly overvalued, but it is not undervalued either. By some multiples, there are discounts to sector averages; by some, on the contrary, high premiums. In my opinion, now is not the level of multiples in order to call the share clearly overvalued, although the shares are trading slightly above the fair price.

Judging by the results of both valuation methods (P/E and EV/EBITDA), the shares are indeed slightly above their fair value. If we take the basic scenario with a 19x P/E multiplier and a 5.5% WACC, we get about $82 per share. The EV/EBITDA method gives the same result: with a 5x multiplier and the same WACC, the price comes out to about $70. All this suggests that the current price already includes the recovery of production, the synergy from the merger, and a strong balance sheet. I maintain a Hold rating with a target price of $82 and a downside of 11%. I do not recommend selling stocks, as I do not see any strong reasons for their fall in price, but short-term corrections of 5-10% are quite likely. At this price, it is much more reasonable to keep stocks in the portfolio and buy them back at drawdowns.

Capital Allocation Focused on Shareholder Returns

The debt burden on the business is low. This allows management to actively pay profits to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. For the entire year 2025, Core has allocated about $245.1 million for repurchase, which is about 6-7% of the total number of shares. On a call with investors and analysts, management made it clear that it would continue to buy shares from the market in 2026. Since February 2025, the company has also started paying dividends of $0.10 per share with a yield of about 0.5% per annum. Yes, so far the dividends are small, but in this regard, the company has a lot to grow, and with good financial results, Core is able to significantly increase the volume of dividends.

Risks

In my opinion, Core is subject to several significant risks that could hurt the company's future earnings if they materialize. A key strategic risk is associated with a long-term decline in interest in coal in the energy sector of the United States and other countries. Yes, demand increased in 2025, but this does not guarantee further growth in the future. If active substitution of coal begins, the Core will suffer greatly from this. Volatility in coal prices can also hit the company's profits and free cash flow. Operational risks related to accidents and stable operation of mines are also always present, and as last year showed, they cannot be ignored. There is also a risk of overestimating the effect of the synergy.

Final Thoughts

In my opinion, the business is balanced in terms of downside risks and growth drivers. In the case of Core, the most effective strategy is to keep stocks in the portfolio (if they have already been purchased) and buy them back on corrections. That's why I keep the Hold rating.

data.title: Telecommunication 2026 Dividend Roundup: I Prefer Comcast Over Verizon
data.description:
VZ vs. CMCSA Stock: Previous Thesis and New Catalysts

Our last analysis for Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) was published on Dec 2, 2025. The article reviewed its FQ3 earnings report and rated it as a hold. As for Comcast Corporation (CMCSA), our last analysis dates back more than 1 year ago. That article was published on Feb 21, 2025, and also rated the stock as a hold. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts surrounding both stocks and also the overall telecommunication sector. The remainder sections of this article will focus on the top 3 developments as I see them: the earnings reports (ER) both companies filed for their fourth fiscal quarter of 2025, their latest dividend declarations, and also the intensification of competitive pressure in the sector. These developments have led me to reevaluate the risk/reward profiles of both VZ and CMCSA, and the assessment has resulted in a rating upgrade for CMCSA to buy.

Given the pronounced role of dividend yield for both companies (and also for telecom stocks in general), I will start with the latest dividend payouts announced by VZ and CMCSA. As seen in the first table below, CMCSA has recently announced its regular quarterly dividend for Q2 2026 in the amount of $0.33 per share with an Ex-Div Date of 4/1/2026. This is unchanged from its payout a year ago and thus unfortunately interrupted the company's dividend growth streak. In the meantime, VZ has also declared its latest payout, also with an Ex-Div Date in early April of 2026. As seen in the next table, VZ declared another quarterly increase at the payout set at $0.7075 per share. This translates into a YOY growth rate of +4.42% from the previous $0.6775 made in April 2025.

1. CMCSA vs. VZ Stock: Dividend Record

Following the wisdom of "dividends don't lie", the rest of this article will examine CMCSA and VZ's Q4 ERs through the window of their last dividend announcements made above. The examination will present a holistic analysis of their Q3 financials following an approach detailed in our earlier articles. This approach is mostly a process developing from our reading of Peter Lynch's various writings. We will keep the background information to a minimum and only provide brief recaps on an as-needed basis.

For stocks where dividends play a large role in their return thesis, Lynch uses consistent payout as an indicator for the stability of their profitability and long-term moat. Telecomm stocks like CMCSA and VZ certainly are good cases to apply this insight. In particular, Lynch prefers to see stocks with stable - or even better, growing - payouts for 20 years or more. With the most recent raise, VZ now meets this requirement with 21 years of consecutive dividend raises, as shown in the chart below. However, as aforementioned, unfortunately, CMCSA's latest payout interrupted the company's 17-year dividend growth streak.

As such, even though - or because of - both CMCSA and VZ having a sufficiently long dividend track record to make dividends a telling indicator of their true economic earnings, VZ certainly has a stronger growth record.

2. CMCSA vs. PEF Stock: Valuation and PEGY Ratios

After establishing the indicative role of dividends for VZ and CMCSA's true earnings, we can move on to using dividend yield and growth for valuation assessment. If you love Lynch's writing and thinking as much as we do, you must be familiar with the following PEGY ratio promoted by him:

PEG refers to the price-to-earnings growth ratio. He also introduced a modified version of the PEG ratio for dividends stocks. The modified ratio is called the PEGY ratio and is computed as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The concept is simple yet effective. If the dividends are high enough, investors should not expect (and do not need either) a high growth rate anymore. Just like the PEG ratio, Lynch favors PEGY ratios below 1x.

Following these insights, the next snapshot displays our estimates of the PEG and also PEGY ratios for CMCSA and VZ stocks. The emphasis is on estimate, and the key source of uncertainty comes from estimating the growth rates, especially with CMCSA pausing its dividend growth. If you recall from the chart below, despite CMCSA's pause, CMCSA has been growing its dividends at a far better pace than VZ in recent years. For example, the dividend growth rate in the past 5 years is 7.85% CAGR for CMCSA and only 1.94% for VZ. Furthermore, with the latest EPS guidance provided in their Q4 ERs, consensus EPS estimates (see the next two charts below) also point to a faster pace of growth for CMCSA on average than for VZ. To wit, CMCSA is projected to grow its EPS at 7.5% CAGR for the next 5 years despite a YOY decline in FY 2026, vs. about 5.96% for VZ.

In our estimates, we used the above consensus projection for their growth rates (i.e., 7.50% for CMCSA and 5.96% for VZ). We felt these estimates were reasonably consistent with their historical trends. With these inputs, VZ's PEGY ratio is 0.90x and CMCSA's PEGY ratio is 0.72x. As such, both ratios are less than 1x, indicating attractive valuation for both stocks. But CMCSA is certainly the more attractive one here with a PEGY ratio that is about 20% lower than VZ's ratio.

3. CMCSA vs. VZ: Dividend Payout Ratios

For all dividend investors, payout ratios are a routine check. On this front, CMCSA features far stronger payout ratios than VZ. If you recall from the first chart, CMCSA's EPS payout ratio is 30.7% per the latest dividend announcement and ER, which is only about ½ of VZ's EPS payout ratio of 58.07%.

To provide a broader perspective, both horizontally and vertically, the next chart compares the cash dividend payout ratios for VZ and CMCSA in the past 5 years, from early 2022 through FQ4 2025. As seen, Verizon's ratio has been consistently above CMCSA. Don't be misled by the data in 2021. VZ's ratio appeared lower that year because it was in the negatives. Overall, you can see that Comcast has maintained a far more consistent and conservative cash payout ratio. Its current reading hovers around the 25% mark, less than ½ of Verizon's 58% reading.

4. CMCSA vs. VZ: Financial Strength

However, the above simple payout ratios can miss some key aspects. For example, they ignore the obligations and assets on a company's balance sheet, which can be of particular interest, especially for telecom stocks given their heavy reliance on leverage. For that reason, the balance sheet strength is our next checkpoint. With the same payout ratios, the company with a less stretched balance sheet obviously provides more dividend safety. On this front, our overall assessment also points to CMCSA as the stronger company than VZ.

To wit, the tables below compare key risk and balance sheet metrics for Verizon and Comcast, offering a detailed look at some of the specific leverage parameters. As seen, Verizon's Total Debt to Equity ratio stands at 174.78%, significantly higher than Comcast's 107.86%. Furthermore, the Altman Z-Score for VZ is 1.27, noticeably below CMCSA's score of 1.45.

5. CMCSA vs. VZ Stock: Profit Margins

Similarly, payout ratios also ignore the role of profit margins. With the same payout ratios, the company with better profit margins can better absorb unexpected headwinds. On this front, both VZ and CMCSA feature equally strong profit margins in my view. To wit, the following chart compares their various profit metrics. Starting with Gross Profit Margin (GPM), VZ's margin is quite robust at 59%, and CMCSA's GPM is even stronger at 71%. Walk down the rest of the income statement; the picture changes slightly from metric to metric, but the overall profile remains very comparable in my eyes. For instance, VZ outshines CMCSA both in terms of EBIT margin (22.9% vs. CMCSA's 16.7%) and also EBITDA margin (36.1% vs. CMCSA's 29.8%). Then in terms of net profit margin, CMCSA's reading of 16.1% is better than VZ's 12.4%. Also note that CMCSA's ROE of 21.4% is also better than VZ's 17% by a noticeable margin.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Finally, judging by the recent price promotions from leading telecom providers, I also sensed a noticeable intensification of the competition pressure in the sector. This has led to various lawsuits among the leading players (and two examples are quoted below) and could continue to pressure the margins for both VZ and CMCSA via more promotions, elevated ad costs, and also additional legal expenses.

Seeking Alpha news (Feb 5): Verizon sues T-Mobile over false ads, hyperbolic savings claims. U.S. telecom Verizon (VZ) filed a lawsuit against rival T-Mobile (TMUS) over false advertising and for causing irreparable harm with misleading claims of yearly savings of over $1,000 if customers switch carriers.

Seeking Alpha news (Mar 2): T-Mobile hits back at Verizon with lawsuit over false 'better deal' ad promise. T-Mobile (TMUS) has sued back Verizon (VZ), accusing its rival of engaging in "bait-and-switch" advertising to make consumers ​switch cellphone carriers.

These uncertainties are why we feel it is important to examine their profits, financial strengths, and dividend safety comprehensively. To recap, this revisit to VZ and CMCSA examined their latest financials through the lens of their dividend declaration for April 2026. CMCSA emerged as the stronger investment candidate in this examination, with the various aspects examined summarized below.
